video.title: Token Maximization Is Over — Remy Guercio, Tailscale & Michael Grinich | AIE World's Fair 2026
video.description:
The last 12 months of AI adoption were about token maximization: stuffing everything into massive context windows because you finally could. Remy Guercio from Tailscale says that era is ending, and ROI maximization is next.

In this conversation, Remy breaks down what he's seeing across companies drowning in AI bills: why consolidating onto a single model provider is the wrong instinct, how open-source and Chinese models are reshaping the cost-per-task curve, and why he thinks about frontier model efficiency the way engineers think about the Concorde, burning more fuel to go twice as fast.

Topics covered:

• Why "token maximization" defined the last year of AI spend
• The wrong way companies are responding to rising AI bills
• Open-weight and Chinese models vs. frontier model efficiency
• The Concorde analogy for test-time compute
• How Tailscale's own engineering team uses AI internally
• What ROI maximization actually looks like in practice

To learn more about Tailscale, visit https://tailscale.com/. To learn more about WorkOS, visit https://workos.com.

session.title: Auth for Agents: Unblock Autonomous AI with auth.md

video.title: The Future of Work: Software Factories, FDEs, and the 46x Curve — 'Job' Is Dissolving
video.description:
Remember when we covered whether AI would wipe out white collar jobs? After listening through a round of interviews from a major AI engineering conference, the question itself has evolved: the frontier companies are busy rewriting the word "job." Work didn't disappear — engineers are splitting into three new forms: on the factory console, forward-deployed at the customer's site, or averaged out in the middle of the 46x curve.

You'll hear:
· Software factories (Warp's CEO): automated systems that continuously triage, implement, review, verify and monitor code changes — humans out of the loop, watching dashboards; his prediction (he sells the factory): within a year most significant projects run one
· Forward deployed engineers (Sierra/Cursor): a role defined by accountability to customer outcomes, not skills; building AI software factories inside customers across the whole SDLC
· The real divide = the unit of work: job vs production line — switch the unit and performance reviews, hiring, and "senior" all need rewriting
· Cursor's cold numbers: weekly output doubled · auto-merged AI code up 5x · ~90 percent of input tokens are context · top developers at 46x (their own sample; read trends)
· The investor seat (Third Point's Dan Loeb, $25B+): die-hard value investors who refuse to evolve are dead — but "human nature is alpha's last moat"
· History rhymes: workshops → assembly lines (craftsmen didn't all lose work; the identity vanished) · Solow's paradox → dark output (a will collapsing from hundreds of dollars to cents, invisible to GDP; the 1990s revision added $3.6T) · white-collar piece-rate risk (unresolved)
· The landing: your performance review sits on a measurement system going blind; three signals to watch over the next two years

Sources: Latent Space @ AI Engineer World's Fair (Zach Lloyd / Natalie Meurer / Pauline Brunet), Cursor's official report, public interviews (relayed, attributed). I relay; I don't referee.
The Next Two Years · Borrow My Brain. Published 2026-07-02.

session.title: How Forward Deployed Engineering is done at Cursor

video.title: Why Trust Is the Bottleneck for Agents — Jean-Denis Greze, Town | AI Engineer World's Fair 2026
video.description:
The default assumption in consumer AI has been that more autonomy is always better: let the agent act, let it send the email, let it book the meeting. Jean-Denis Greze, founder of Town and former CTO of Plaid, says that's backwards. The products that win will be the ones that earn autonomy slowly.

In this conversation, Jean-Denis breaks down how Town decided AI should never be allowed to send an email, only draft one: why the company pivoted from AI tax prep to a personal assistant after watching preparers drown in email and scheduling, how Town graduates an automation from "draft only" to "just handle it" only after a user proves they trust it, and why he thinks the inbox itself disappears as agents get better at filtering it. He also lays out a vision for agent-to-agent collaboration across companies, where LLMs share relationship and institutional signals without ever exposing anyone's raw inbox.

Topics covered:

• Why Town restricts its AI to drafts, never sends
• The pivot from AI tax prep to a personal assistant for everyone
• How Town's automations earn more trust and autonomy over time
• Why "not making the user look stupid" is a non-negotiable design rule
• What happens to the inbox once an agent is filtering it well
• Agent-to-agent collaboration and data silos across a company

To learn more about Town, visit www.town.com. To learn more about WorkOS, visit https://workos.com.

- 如果您有任何问题或者建议，欢迎通过邮件联系我：0001coder@gmail.com

AI Engineer World's Fair 2026 6月29日在旧金山开幕，566 场议题、160+ 场谈 Agent。我挑了五场关于 Agent 架构的 talk，提前分享核心观点 —— 共识很清楚：模型在变强，瓶颈在架构。

本期五场：
- ① 架构的保质期只有 6 个月（Dan Farrelly / Inngest）：与其追范式，不如打磨稳定的原始构件，换模型应像换数据库驱动一样简单
- ② 不是 Agent 失败，是 Harness 失败（Vinoth Govindarajan / OpenAI）：大量生产故障在模型外面那圈系统 —— 模型提出、Harness 提交、收据证明；用 OpenClaw 做公开案例
- ③ 长期 Agent 的记忆架构（Stefania Druga / Sakana AI）：紧约束命题 —— 三层记忆、渐进式披露、先召回后压缩、子 agent 隔离
- ④ 修生产事故的六根支柱（Varun Krovvidi / Resolve AI）：编排 / 上下文 / 推理 / 行动 / 学习 / 评估，更好的模型不会让任何一根消失
- ⑤ Prompt · Memory · Weights（Anant Srivastava / Oracle）：三条路不是梯子，是互补通道，最后形成循环

video.title: AIE World's Fair 2026 - Day 3 in 13 min -  AI Engineer - WF2026
video.description:
The president of Y Combinator told 7,000 engineers: "The 2x people and the 100x people are using the exact same model. The leverage is not in the weights. It's in how you wire the work."

That was the closing day of the AI Engineer World's Fair — and underneath it is a build-vs-rent question every operator is about to face.

The whole day converged on one line: the model is rented. What you own is the moat.

→ Garry Tan (YC): the harness IS an org chart. A skill file is an employee; the resolver table is the org chart. "You're not writing software. You're hiring, training, and managing a workforce made of markdown."

→ Survey of 1,048 engineers: 95% now use agents, and agent write-access jumped 52% → 89% in a single year. Nobody has settled the control layer.

→ The security rule everyone repeated: "if it isn't enforced deterministically, it is not enforced." PostHog runs a scanner with no model anywhere on the blocking path. Salesforce: "enforce isolation in your infrastructure, never in your prompts."

→ Memory is the open frontier: "model quality is rented — but if you build your brain, you own that brain. If you don't own your harness, you don't own your memory."

This is the case for owning your stack instead of renting someone else's — which is exactly why we run our own inference, our own knowledge graph, and our own harness.

A 13-minute breakdown, every claim tied to its speaker. Link in the comments.

If build-vs-rent for your AI stack is on your desk, send this to whoever owns that call.

#AIEngineering #SovereignAI #AIAgents

---
## Posting notes
- **Image:** the video thumbnail (`03-...-thumb.png`, "THE MODEL IS RENTED / 400x IS REAL").
- **Link:** YouTube URL in the FIRST COMMENT, not the body.
- **Signals targeted:** sends + profile-views (build-vs-rent persona handle; this is the closest post to our sovereign-AI consulting thesis — the best lead-gen of the three), saves (survey stats + enforcement rule).
- **Frame:** own-vs-rent moat (distinct from Day 1/2). The one that bridges hardest to CorticalFlow — keep the bridge to the single capability-voice line, don't turn it into a pitch.

video.title: AIE World's Fair 2026 - Day 2 in 13 min -  AI Engineer - WF2026
video.description:
Day 2 of AI Engineer World's Fair 2026 had a theme that sounds like science fiction: autoresearch. The idea that the machine can now run its own experiments and improve itself. For decades, building AI was something humans did to models — this was the day the field started handing that job to the models themselves.

We compressed the entire Day-2 main stage into the findings that matter: why the models are "grown, not designed," why Google DeepMind says "code is over" and the answer is AlphaZero-style self-play, how GEPA evolves prompts and whole agent harnesses in text space (beating 25,000 RL rollouts with three examples), agents that reproduce GPT-2 overnight and beat ML speedruns on their own, evals as the fitness function ("agent as a judge"), the Karpathy-named "auto-research layer," why the OODA loop from 1970s dogfighting is the native shape of an agent, and the strange economics underneath it all.

Built from the complete Day-2 main-stage transcript, processed through CorticalFlow's research pipeline — every claim in this video is attributed to the person who said it on stage.

⏱ Chapters

0:41  Models are grown, not designed
1:30  A field guide to a model you have to discover

video.title: IRL at AI Engineer World's Fair in SF
video.description:
We are live and in real life on the floor of the AI Engineer World's Fair in San Francisco. Day one, walking around to find out what everyone is actually building.

This is the largest technical conference for AI engineers in the world. Thousands of people, the top labs, and a lot of the companies you already use, all in one building at Moscone West. I have a camera and a badge, and I am roaming the floor, stopping at booths, catching a few of the talks, and talking to the people doing the work.

The theme I keep hearing on day one is software factories. Agents writing, testing, and shipping software at scale, and what agentic engineering actually looks like once it leaves the demo. I want to see how real that is up close.

Heads up that this is IRL, so it is touch and go. The signal will drop, the audio will get loud, and we will probably run into a few surprise guests. That is the fun of it. Come hang out, tell me where you are watching from, and tell me who you want me to track down on the floor.

We are streaming all week, so if you are here in SF, come say hi.

Join the community in Skool: https://rfer.me/startmy.ai
Where are you watching from? Drop it in the chat.

video.title: AIE World's Fair 2026 - Day 1 in 13 min -  AI Engineer - WF2026
video.description:
7,000 engineers packed into San Francisco for the opening day of the biggest AI engineering event ever held — and the theme was a strange one: software factories. The idea that building software is becoming an industrial process, where agents do the work while you sleep. It's a thrilling promise with a catch the whole day kept circling back to: a factory can build real software, or it can mass-produce garbage. The industry has a word for that garbage. Slop.

We compressed the entire Day-1 main stage of AI Engineer World's Fair 2026 — the "Software Factories" day — into the findings that matter: why everything is loops now, how OpenAI, Microsoft and the open-weight labs are reframing the work, the 100-person factory in India running a named swarm of agents, the war on slop, the counter-thesis that "the harness is not enough," the deterministic rails going in underneath, and the strangest idea of all — models that are learning to train the next model.

Built from the complete Day-1 main-stage transcript, processed through CorticalFlow's research pipeline — every claim in this video is attributed to the person who said it on stage.

⏱ Chapters

10:10 What Day 1 adds up to

🧠 Powered by CorticalFlow
Powered by CorticalFlow's private intelligence stack — a self-hosted research engine that ingests conferences, filings, broadcasts and supply-chain data into a unified knowledge graph, then synthesizes the structural picture you can't get from headlines. Built for operators who need the map underneath the noise. We don't just summarize — we attribute every claim to its source and surface what the consensus is missing.

video.title: I Went to the Biggest AI Conference in the World. Only 5 Startups Impressed Me.
video.description:
The AI Engineer World Fair had 236 AI companies last week in San Francisco. Many were hype. 5 were especially worth knowing.

 👉🏻 PLEASE READ: This is a completely independent, unsponsored video about the AI companies that genuinely impressed me (which is rare, because I’m pretty slow to endorse anything in the AI hype cycle). No company paid for placement. 

The @aiDotEngineer World Fair is considered the largest AI conference in the world, covering everything from Agentic AI, Unitree robotics AI development tools to AGI. But you didn’t need to be there to understand what’s happening deep in Silicon Valley. I’ll break it down for you in 10 minutes.
_____________________________________________________________________________________

Hi all, I'm Veronica. 🥰 I've been in software for 10 years, AI for 6 starting on AI combat ships for the military. I sit in the middle of the AI hype cycle and report both sides (the good and the bad).

I also gave my own talk at the AIEWF. Watch here 👉🏻https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d_Ftrl3vfV0

video.title: GPT-5.6 STILL LOCKED + Nobel Laureate Switches Labs + Colorado AI Law Gutted | AI News June 29, 2026
video.description:
Today's AI news for business operators — full analysis at https://bizthrive.ai

📋 CHAPTERS
0:00 Intro — Government-gated AI access is the new normal
0:35 Story 1: AI Engineer World's Fair 2026 kicks off in San Francisco
1:47 Story 2: GPT-5.6 Sol still locked — broad access estimated July 10-17
3:03 Story 3: Trump's AI Executive Order — the legal framework behind model restrictions
4:41 Story 4: Nobel laureate John Jumper leaves DeepMind for Anthropic
6:11 Story 5: Colorado's AI Act replaced before it could take effect
7:41 Story 6: Qualcomm in $10B talks for Tenstorrent — RISC-V challenge to Nvidia
9:07 Story 7: SpaceX signs $6.3B compute deal — neoclouds are here
10:34 Business Takeaway — models, compute, and regulation: the three control points
11:43 Outro

📰 TODAY'S STORIES

The AI Engineer World's Fair 2026 opens today at Moscone West in San Francisco — 6,000+ engineers, 300 speakers, 29 tracks. The largest technical AI conference in the world, built for builders shipping AI applications.

GPT-5.6 Sol remains restricted to ~20 government-approved companies three days after launch. No public waitlist, no ChatGPT access. Broad availability estimated July 10-17. The first US frontier model launched under a government-curated access list.

Trump's June 2 Executive Order on AI creates a voluntary 30-day pre-deployment review framework for frontier models, due by August 1. Technically voluntary, but practically being followed — this is the legal basis for GPT-5.6 and Mythos restrictions.

Nobel laureate John Jumper (AlphaFold 2, 2024 Chemistry Nobel) leaves Google DeepMind after 9 years for Anthropic. Follows Anthropic's $400M acquisition of Coefficient Bio. DeepMind-to-Anthropic talent flow is 11:1.

Colorado's AI Act — the first comprehensive state AI law — was replaced before its June 30 effective date after White House pressure and a federal court stay. Federal policy will set US AI rules, not state patchworks.

Qualcomm in advanced talks to acquire Tenstorrent (Jim Keller's RISC-V AI chip startup) for $8-10B. Valuation tripled in 12 months. Could break Nvidia's AI compute monopoly.

SpaceX signs $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI — $150M/month for GB300 access at Colossus 2. SpaceX now hosts Anthropic ($1.25B/mo), Google ($920M/mo), and Reflection. Neoclouds are becoming the AI infrastructure layer.

🔗 Resources: https://bizthrive.ai

#AINews #ArtificialIntelligence #AIForBusiness #OpenAI #GPT56 #Anthropic #JohnJumper #Qualcomm #Tenstorrent #SpaceX #AIRegulation #ColoradoAIAct #BizThriveAI #AIStrategy #AIEngineer
